title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a marine biologist called Libby Vance talking about her experiences exploring the deep ocean in a submersible. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a marine biologist called Libby Vance talking about her experiences exploring the deep ocean in a submersible. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Libby Vance describes the biological wonders and technical difficulties of working in the abyss.
extracts.audio_script:
Good morning. I’m Libby. Most of our planet is covered by water, yet we know more about the surface of the Moon than we do about the bottom of the Atlantic. I’m a deep-sea biologist. My interest in the abyss wasn't sparked by a nature documentary; it actually began when I found a [7]strange glowing jellyfish[/7] washed up on a beach in Scotland when I was seven years old. I couldn't understand how something so beautiful could exist in the dark, cold water. That curiosity has defined my entire adult life.

Going down to the ocean floor is a journey into another world. As you descend, the light fades until you are in total darkness. The pressure is immense—equivalent to having an elephant stand on your thumb. Because of this, our submersibles are made of thick titanium. I’ve found that the [8]window thickness[/8] is the most claustrophobic part of the experience. You are looking at the world through several inches of acrylic glass, which slightly distorts the colors of the creatures outside. It’s a constant reminder of the hostile environment just inches away.

Down there, life doesn't rely on the sun. Instead, entire ecosystems survive on 'marine snow'—tiny particles of organic matter drifting down from the surface. However, the most spectacular sights are around hydrothermal vents. I was amazed to find that [9]giant tubeworms[/9] thrive in these boiling, toxic waters. They don't have mouths or stomachs; they rely on a symbiotic relationship with bacteria to turn chemicals into energy. It’s a completely alien way of life that challenged everything we thought we knew about biology.

Collecting samples at these depths is incredibly delicate work. We use robotic arms controlled from inside the sub. I’ve noticed that the [10]sediment cloud[/10] created by the thrusters is our biggest operational headache. One wrong move and you are blinded by a thick veil of mud that can take twenty minutes to settle. You need incredible patience and a very steady hand to be a deep-sea pilot.

One of the most concerning things I’ve witnessed in recent years is the presence of human impact even in the deepest trenches. I recently spotted a [11]plastic shopping bag[/12] at a depth of seven thousand meters. It was a heartbreaking moment. It shows that our waste reaches places that humans have never even seen in person. It has made me realize that my job isn't just about discovery, but also about advocacy for ocean protection.

In terms of equipment, we are seeing a shift towards autonomous vehicles. While they are useful, I believe that the [12]human presence[/12] is still vital for making split-second scientific observations. A machine can follow a programmed path, but it can't feel the 'hunch' that a particular rock formation might be hiding a new species. There is an emotional connection to discovery that you only get when you are physically there.

Looking forward, I’m worried about the prospect of deep-sea mining. Companies are interested in the mineral-rich rocks found on the seabed. I believe that a [13]global moratorium[/13] is necessary until we fully understand the impact this would have on the fragile deep-sea food web. We risk destroying species before we’ve even named them.

Finally, I’m currently working on a [14]virtual reality film[/14] that will allow students to experience a dive for themselves. I want to inspire the next generation to look down at the ocean with the same wonder they look up at the stars. Thank you for your time.
